refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by the tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability, and (2) ensuring latency is compatible with safety. The refined version separates the functional intent (enable teleoperation communication) from the performance constraint (latency compatible with safe operation). The FR now resides purely in the problem domain—it specifies what must be achieved (teleoperation with acceptable latency) without prescribing how (e.g., network protocol, compression, edge computing). The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is a quantified constraint that flows from this FR and is verified separately.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (sensor input to actuator command output) under agreed network conditions. Confirm p95 latency ≤ 150 ms. Conduct operational trials with tele-operator to validate that latency permits safe task execution (e.g., precision placement, emergency stop responsiveness).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall acquire and transmit tele-operator control inputs to the remote equipment.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall acquire and transmit equipment state and sensor feedback to the tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain end-to-end communication latency within limits that preserve safe remote operation.

candidate_dps: DP1: Establish a dedicated, low-latency communication pathway with end-to-end latency monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization to ensure operator commands and telemetry reach within the p95 ≤ 150 ms budget.

DP2: Implement a hierarchical control architecture where local autonomous safety logic operates independently of teleoperation link, with teleoperation commands treated as supervisory inputs that are validated against local safety constraints before execution.
DP3: Design a redundant communication substrate with automatic failover and link-quality detection, allowing the system to gracefully degrade or halt operations if latency exceeds safe thresholds.
DP4: Employ edge-local command buffering and predictive state synchronization to mask transient network jitter, ensuring operator perception of responsiveness even under variable network conditions.

DP5: Establish a formal latency budget allocation across communication layers (encoding, transmission, decoding, processing) with per-layer monitoring and adaptive codec/compression selection to stay within p95 ≤ 150 ms.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2 + DP3 + DP5 (integrated approach): Implement a hierarchical control architecture where local autonomous safety logic operates independently of the teleoperation link, supported by a redundant communication substrate with automatic failover and link-quality detection. Allocate and monitor a formal latency budget across communication layers (encoding, transmission, decoding, processing) with adaptive codec selection and per-layer instrumentation. Teleoperation commands are treated as supervisory inputs validated against local safety constraints before execution.

potential_sources: DP2 (local safety logic) and DP3 (failover) are tightly coupled by design—failover must trigger safety-mode transitions; this is intentional and acceptable.
DP5 (latency budget) depends on DP3 (link quality detection) to inform adaptive codec selection; this is a logical dependency, not a problematic coupling.

Risk: DP2 assumes local safety logic can operate independently; if safety rules require real-time operator input (e.g., dynamic hazard assessment), this assumption breaks. Mitigation: Clarify safety plan to define which decisions are autonomous vs. operator-supervised.
coupling_check.notes: The three DPs form a coherent solution: hierarchical control decouples safety from communication reliability, redundancy ensures availability, and latency budgeting ensures performance. No circular dependencies or hidden cross-domain assumptions detected. Coupling is intentional and architecturally sound.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: 'Agreed network conditions' are well-defined and stable (e.g., bandwidth, jitter, packet loss rates). Risk: If conditions are vague or variable, latency budget allocation (DP5) becomes unreliable. Mitigation: Require explicit network SLA or characterization before design finalization.

Assumption: Local autonomous safety logic can be implemented and validated independently of teleoperation. Risk: If safety rules are operator-dependent or context-sensitive, DP2 fails. Mitigation: Worksite safety plan must explicitly partition autonomous vs. supervised decisions.

Assumption: Failover and link-quality detection (DP3) can be implemented with sufficient reliability and speed. Risk: Failover latency itself could exceed the 150 ms budget if not carefully designed. Mitigation: Include failover latency in the budget allocation (DP5).

Assumption: Operator can safely respond to latency-induced delays or system state divergence. Risk: If 150 ms p95 is insufficient for safe operator reaction time, the FR itself is unachievable. Mitigation: Validate p95 ≤ 150 ms against human factors and worksite hazard analysis.

Assumption: Adaptive codec/compression (DP5) does not introduce additional latency or quality degradation that undermines safety. Risk: Codec switching overhead or lossy compression could degrade situational awareness. Mitigation: Define codec selection rules and test under worst-case network conditions.

Assumption: Redundant communication substrate (DP3) is cost-effective and operationally feasible. Risk: Multiple communication links may be impractical or expensive in remote worksites. Mitigation: Clarify redundancy scope (e.g., dual uplinks vs. dual full stacks) early.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by the tele-operator, such that: (1) p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions; (2) local autonomous safety logic operates independently and halts operations if teleoperation link quality degrades below defined thresholds; (3) operator commands are validated against real-time safety constraints before execution; (4) system state and operator perception remain synchronized within acceptable bounds even under transient network jitter. Acceptance criteria: p95 latency ≤ 150 ms; failover time ≤ [X] ms; link-quality detection sensitivity ≤ [Y] ms; codec adaptation overhead ≤ [Z] ms. Dependencies: Worksite safety plan must define autonomous vs. operator-supervised decisions; network SLA must specify bandwidth, jitter, and packet loss; human factors analysis must validate 150 ms as safe for operator reaction time.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for hierarchical safety logic, latency budget management, communication redundancy coordination, and validation of teleoperation commands against local safety constraints. Owns the formal safety state machine, latency instrumentation, codec adaptation logic, and failover orchestration.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives teleoperation commands from remote operator; Safety Services validates these against local safety constraints before forwarding to Vehicle execution layer.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits safety violations, latency budget exceedances, communication link degradation, and failover events to operator and logging systems.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Interfaces with redundant communication substrate; Safety Services monitors link quality, triggers failover, selects adaptive codecs, and instruments per-layer latency measurements.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives vehicle state and sensor data from Vehicle module; used to validate command feasibility and update local safety constraints.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural owner of hierarchical safety logic, latency budget tracking, and command validation. Redundancy coordination and codec adaptation are safety-critical concerns. Minor diffusion: Network Link interface implementation details (physical redundancy switching) may reside in Vehicle or NGHE Solution, but orchestration logic stays in Safety Services.

Poor fit. Vehicle is primarily an execution layer for commands post-validation. Placing hierarchical safety logic, latency budgeting, and teleoperation validation here violates separation of concerns. Safety logic would diffuse across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules. Vehicle should receive pre-validated commands only.

Weak fit. Teleoperation handles operator interface and command transmission, but should not own safety validation or latency budget enforcement. Placing safety logic here creates tight coupling between operator interface and safety constraints, reducing modularity and testability. Safety logic would diffuse to Vehicle and Safety Services.

Poor fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level integration container. Placing detailed hierarchical safety logic, latency instrumentation, and codec adaptation here violates layering principles and makes the solution-level module implementation-specific. These concerns should be encapsulated in a dedicated Safety Services module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most cohesive and least diffuse boundary for the integrated DP (hierarchical safety + latency budgeting + redundancy + command validation). This module naturally owns the formal safety state machine, latency instrumentation across all communication layers, adaptive codec selection logic, and the validation gate that ensures teleoperation commands respect local safety constraints before Vehicle execution. Redundant communication substrate orchestration (failover, link-quality detection) is a safety-critical concern and belongs in Safety Services; physical implementation of redundancy (e.g., dual network interfaces) may reside in Vehicle or NGHE Solution infrastructure, but the decision logic and monitoring stay in Safety Services.
containment_risks: Risk: If Network Link interface is too tightly coupled to specific hardware redundancy implementations (e.g., dual Ethernet, CAN failover), Safety Services may become hardware-dependent. Mitigation: Define Network Link as an abstract interface that abstracts redundancy switching; Vehicle or NGHE Solution provides the concrete implementation.

Risk: Latency instrumentation across encoding, transmission, decoding, and processing layers requires hooks in multiple modules (Teleoperation, Vehicle, Network Link). If these hooks are not cleanly defined, Safety Services may become invasive. Mitigation: Define a standardized latency telemetry contract (e.g., timestamp + layer ID) that all modules emit; Safety Services aggregates and enforces budget.

Risk: Codec adaptation logic may require knowledge of Vehicle capabilities (e.g., supported compression formats). If Vehicle module does not expose this capability clearly, Safety Services must query Vehicle repeatedly, creating coupling. Mitigation: Vehicle exposes a capability interface listing supported codecs and their latency profiles; Safety Services queries once at initialization.

Risk: Command validation against local safety constraints requires real-time access to vehicle state. If Telemetry interface is high-latency or asynchronous, validation may operate on stale state. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry is a high-priority, low-latency interface; Safety Services maintains a local cache of vehicle state with age tracking.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP required. The selected DP (hierarchical safety + latency budgeting + redundancy + command validation) is well-suited to Safety Services containment. However, to improve clarity and reduce diffusion risk, recommend refining the DP to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Safety Services owns hierarchical safety logic, latency budget enforcement, and command validation; (2) Vehicle owns local autonomous execution and state management; (3) Network Link (owned by NGHE Solution or Vehicle) owns physical redundancy switching and link monitoring; (4) Teleoperation owns operator interface and command transmission. This layering ensures Safety Services remains focused on safety-critical logic without becoming entangled in network hardware or vehicle execution details.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR requirement (safe remote operation with latency ≤ 150 ms p95) is directly traceable to Safety Services IM boundary through: (1) latency budget instrumentation across encoding/transmission/decoding/processing layers with per-layer telemetry contract; (2) formal safety state machine and command validation logic; (3) SafetyAlert interface for latency exceedance reporting. Acceptance criterion is measurable via aggregated latency telemetry from all modules feeding Safety Services. Vehicle state telemetry interface ensures validation operates on current state. Failover and link-quality detection provide the redundancy substrate required for safe operation under network degradation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Safety Services owns the critical path: command reception → validation against local safety constraints → latency budget enforcement → execution gate. Telemetry input ensures state freshness. Network Link interface abstracts physical redundancy, preventing Safety Services from becoming hardware-dependent. Teleoperation and Vehicle modules have well-defined pre/post-validation responsibilities. Risk: Telemetry interface latency and cache staleness could undermine validation accuracy; mitigation (high-priority, low-latency Telemetry + age tracking) is specified but requires implementation verification.

diffusion_risks: Latency instrumentation diffusion: Encoding/transmission/decoding/processing layer hooks must be implemented across Teleoperation, Network Link, and Vehicle modules. If standardized telemetry contract (timestamp + layer ID) is not enforced, Safety Services becomes invasive and tightly coupled. Mitigation: Define and enforce a formal latency telemetry schema; all modules emit structured events; Safety Services aggregates only.

Codec adaptation coupling: Safety Services must query Vehicle for supported codecs and latency profiles. If Vehicle does not expose a stable capability interface at initialization, Safety Services will incur repeated queries and runtime coupling. Mitigation: Vehicle provides immutable codec capability manifest at startup; Safety Services caches and uses for adaptation decisions.

Network Link abstraction leakage: Physical redundancy switching (dual Ethernet, CAN failover) may be implemented in Vehicle or NGHE Solution infrastructure. If Safety Services must know specific hardware details to orchestrate failover, abstraction breaks. Mitigation: Network Link interface defines abstract failover commands (e.g., 'switch_to_backup', 'monitor_link_quality'); concrete implementation hidden from Safety Services.

Telemetry staleness under high load: If Vehicle state updates are asynchronous or batched, Safety Services may validate commands against stale state, creating a safety window. Mitigation: Telemetry interface must guarantee bounded latency (e.g., ≤ 50 ms age); Safety Services rejects commands if state age exceeds threshold.

Failover orchestration timing: Automatic failover triggered by Safety Services must complete within latency budget. If failover decision logic is correct but physical switching is slow, the DP fails. Mitigation: Measure end-to-end failover latency (decision + physical switch + link re-establishment); ensure p95 ≤ 50 ms to leave margin for command processing within 150 ms budget.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Safety Services is the natural owner of hierarchical safety logic, latency budget tracking, command validation, and failover orchestration. The module boundary is cohesive and avoids scattering safety-critical logic across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or NGHE Solution. Minor diffusion risks are well-characterized and mitigable through interface contracts (latency telemetry schema, codec capability manifest, abstract Network Link interface, Telemetry age tracking). Recommended refinement: Explicitly document the latency telemetry contract (schema, emission frequency, layer IDs) and the Network Link abstraction (failover commands, link-quality metrics) as part of Safety Services interface specification. This prevents diffusion at implementation time.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP (hierarchical safety + latency budgeting + redundancy + command validation) directly addresses the FR requirement for safe remote operation with bounded latency. Decomposition is sound: (1) Hierarchical safety logic ensures local autonomous operation independent of teleoperation link, satisfying safety under link failure; (2) Formal latency budget allocation across communication layers with per-layer instrumentation enables p95 ≤ 150 ms verification; (3) Redundant communication substrate with automatic failover and link-quality detection provides resilience; (4) Command validation against local safety constraints ensures teleoperation inputs respect vehicle state and safety boundaries. The DP is implementable, testable, and traceable to acceptance criteria. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the latency telemetry contract: Define a schema for per-layer latency events (timestamp, layer_id, latency_ms, codec_type, link_quality_metric). Require all modules (Teleoperation, Network Link, Vehicle) to emit events conforming to this schema. Safety Services aggregates and enforces budget. Document in Safety Services interface specification.

Define Network Link abstraction explicitly: Specify abstract failover commands (e.g., 'switch_to_backup_link', 'get_link_quality', 'set_codec') and their latency SLAs. Hide physical redundancy implementation (dual Ethernet, CAN, etc.) behind this interface. Vehicle or NGHE Solution provides concrete implementation; Safety Services calls abstract interface only.

Establish Telemetry interface SLA: Guarantee vehicle state update frequency (e.g., ≥ 20 Hz) and maximum age (e.g., ≤ 50 ms). Safety Services rejects commands if state age exceeds threshold. Document in Telemetry interface specification.

Measure end-to-end failover latency: Add acceptance criterion: 'Failover decision + physical switch + link re-establishment shall complete in ≤ 50 ms p95 to preserve 150 ms command latency budget.' Instrument Safety Services and Network Link to measure and report failover latency.

Clarify codec capability exposure: Vehicle shall expose a static codec capability manifest at initialization (supported formats, latency profiles, compression ratios). Safety Services queries once and caches. Document in Vehicle interface specification to prevent runtime coupling.

Add latency budget allocation table: Specify budget breakdown (e.g., encoding ≤ 20 ms, transmission ≤ 80 ms, decoding ≤ 20 ms, processing ≤ 30 ms = 150 ms total). Use this to set per-layer thresholds and trigger adaptive codec selection when any layer approaches its budget. Document in Safety Services design specification.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with high confidence, and containment is excellent (0.92). However, the recommended_revisions list identifies six critical interface and contract specifications that must be formalized before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. These are not logical gaps in the FR-DP-IM mapping, but rather essential architectural clarifications required to prevent diffusion risks at implementation time. Given the high safety criticality of this system, formalizing these contracts at the current level is a prerequisite to sound sub-FR derivation. Proceeding to decomposition without these specifications would risk scattering safety-critical logic across modules at lower levels due to ambiguous interface boundaries.

revise.why: The DP is logically sound and addresses the FR completely. However, the design specification must be enriched with six formal interface contracts before sub-FR decomposition: (1) Latency telemetry schema and emission contract; (2) Network Link abstraction interface with failover commands and SLAs; (3) Telemetry interface SLA (update frequency, maximum age, rejection logic); (4) End-to-end failover latency acceptance criterion (≤ 50 ms p95); (5) Codec capability manifest exposure and caching protocol; (6) Latency budget allocation table with per-layer thresholds and adaptive codec selection triggers. These contracts are part of the DP specification and must be documented before deriving sub-FRs for Safety Services, Network Link, and Vehicle modules. This ensures that sub-level decomposition operates within well-defined interface boundaries and prevents safety-critical logic diffusion.

assumptions: Latency telemetry instrumentation can be implemented across Teleoperation, Network Link, and Vehicle modules without introducing unacceptable performance overhead.
Vehicle can expose a static codec capability manifest at initialization that remains valid for the duration of the teleoperation session.
Network Link abstraction can hide physical redundancy details (dual Ethernet, CAN failover) behind abstract failover commands without loss of safety guarantees.
Telemetry interface can guarantee vehicle state update frequency ≥ 20 Hz with maximum age ≤ 50 ms under nominal and degraded network conditions.
Failover decision logic in Safety Services can complete within ≤ 50 ms p95 to preserve the 150 ms command latency budget.
Adaptive codec selection based on per-layer latency thresholds can be implemented without introducing race conditions or state inconsistencies.
open_risks: Latency telemetry schema enforcement: If modules emit telemetry events with inconsistent timestamps or layer IDs, Safety Services aggregation becomes unreliable. Mitigation requires strict schema validation and per-module testing.

Telemetry staleness under high load: Vehicle state updates may batch or delay under high CPU load, causing Safety Services to validate against stale state. Requires bounded-latency guarantee in Telemetry interface SLA and runtime age-checking logic.

Failover orchestration timing: Physical link switching (dual Ethernet, CAN) may exceed 50 ms p95 latency budget, causing end-to-end failover to exceed 150 ms command budget. Requires measurement and potential hardware optimization.

Codec capability manifest immutability: If Vehicle codec capabilities change during session (e.g., due to firmware update or link degradation), cached manifest becomes stale. Requires protocol for manifest invalidation and refresh.

Network Link abstraction leakage: Concrete failover implementation may expose hardware-specific details (e.g., CAN bus arbitration delays) that Safety Services must account for. Requires careful abstraction design and latency profiling.

Diffusion of latency instrumentation: If latency telemetry hooks are not standardized, each module may implement custom instrumentation, leading to inconsistent data and Safety Services coupling. Requires formal schema and enforcement.
evidence_needed: Formal latency telemetry schema specification (JSON/Protobuf) with timestamp precision, layer ID enumeration, codec type, link quality metrics, and emission frequency requirements.
Network Link interface specification document defining abstract failover commands (switch_to_backup_link, get_link_quality, set_codec), their latency SLAs, and error handling semantics.
Telemetry interface SLA specification guaranteeing vehicle state update frequency (≥ 20 Hz), maximum age (≤ 50 ms), and Safety Services rejection logic for stale state.
End-to-end failover latency measurement plan and acceptance criterion (≤ 50 ms p95) with instrumentation points in Safety Services and Network Link modules.
Vehicle codec capability manifest schema and initialization protocol, including manifest versioning and refresh mechanism.
Latency budget allocation table with per-layer thresholds (encoding ≤ 20 ms, transmission ≤ 80 ms, decoding ≤ 20 ms, processing ≤ 30 ms) and adaptive codec selection decision logic.
Proof-of-concept or prototype demonstrating latency telemetry aggregation, failover orchestration, and codec adaptation within specified budgets under nominal and degraded network conditions.
Safety analysis (FMEA or FTA) of latency telemetry schema violations, Telemetry staleness, and failover timing failures, with mitigation strategies.
